dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for chest pain.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Chest pain and shortness of breath started about 30 minutes ago after coming home from the store.
You're alert, awake, and responding to questions clearly. How's your mental status now?
You appear alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Do you understand me right now?
I'm fine now, but I was in a lot of pain when it started.
You consistently opened your eyes to verbal stimuli with no delay during our assessment.
I noted earlier that you moved both arms and legs when prompted with no deficits.
Your airway is patent and unobstructed at all times. There was no intervention required.
Your breathing is normal in rate, rhythm, and depth. You experienced some shortness of breath with the initial pain.
Can I ask, did you ever feel out of breath before the chest pain began?
Yeah, my breathing was normal before the chest pain, but it felt worse when the pain hit.
Got it. Your work of breathing was within expected limits during the examination. Any wheezes or abnormal sounds?
Your radial pulses are weak but equal on both sides. Skin on your upper arms and torso is warm and dry.
Your hands are a bit cool with capillary refill over 4 seconds. You mentioned you were outside cold without gloves.
Yes, it was very cold, and I didn't wear gloves walking home.
Your mental status is alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Have you had any confusion or strange behavior today?
No, I stayed alert the entire time. Never lost awareness.
Your HEENT exam is normocephalic with no deformities. We noted no excess discharge or secretions.
Your pupils are 3mm, round, equal, and reactive to light. Any headache or visual changes?
No, I didn't have a headache or visual oddities. It was just my chest discomfort.
Got it. We'll chart you as reporting no headache.
Chest rise and fall are within normal limits. We found your breath sounds equal and clear.
No chest wall tenderness is noted, and no wounds or bruising on your chest.
I don't see any bruising and my chest feels fine now.
Your abdomen is soft with minimal tenderness at baseline. Any abdominal pain recently?
No, not during the chest incident. But two weeks ago I was in the hospital for some GI investigations.
Was there suspicion of a gastrointestinal bleed at that time?
Yes, the doctors looked for that, but didn't find anything.
Your extremities move normally. Any unusual swelling or edema in your legs today?
When the chest pain came on, you described a sensation of shortness of breath, correct?
Yes, I felt out of breath during the event.
Do you normally experience swelling or edema in your legs besides your heart condition?
Any use of alcohol or recreational drugs in the past week, such as drinking or smoking?
No, I haven't consumed either recently.
Have you had any numbness, tingling, or abnormal feelings in your arms or legs during your pain episode?
No, never any tingling or numbness. Only chest pain and breathing difficulty.
Any fever, chills, or sweats in the last 24 hours?
No, only the diaphoresis that came with the chest pain.
The pain you described was sharp, localized to the left chest, with severity of 7/10.
Yes, it was constant up until it went away.
You mentioned your known heart problems: heart failure, hypertension, and an automatic defibrillator. Correct?
Yes, I have all of those diagnoses.
You have no drug allergies but take multiple medications for your heart and blood pressure.
Let's confirm these medications for the hospital in our pre-arrival report.
When the pain was at its worst, would you describe it as sharp or more like a pressure?
Definitely sharp, pinching. It localized well in the left side.
Worse with any movement or deep breathing?
No, just when I exerted myself like walking up a hill.
The pain became non-constant and resolved itself. Did it get worse during any rest time?
I noticed when you were resting on the couch, you were no longer in pain or discomfort.
Your pulse was 70 at arrival and 68 during transport.
Your respiratory rate remained steady at 16 after checking multiple times.
Your initial systolic was 124 and later was recorded at 120.
Your glucose was 129 at first but wasn't checked after.
Oxygen levels dropped from 96% at first down to an unmeasurable value, but you weren't receiving oxygen.
The EKG showed a normal sinus rhythm from our monitoring during transport.
We started 4-lead ECG monitoring and then completed a 12-lead ECG, showing no acute changes.
Capnography was done and returned no signs of airway issues or decreases in perfusion.
The EKG results indicate nothing new from your implanted device or standard leads on your chest.
We made an attempt to place an IV, but were unable to complete the procedure.
You had a GCS of 15 as evidence that you were alert, oriented and well spoken during our initial evaluation.
Your LVAD was assessed, showing fully charged batteries with no alarming signals.
Your condition was reassessed every 5 minutes, with no change in your breathing or strength noted.
Vital signs have remained stable throughout the transport with consistent readings.
Continuous monitoring confirmed a normal sinus rhythm with no new arrhythmias.
We are preparing you for transport to St. Mary's Hospital in Richmond.
You're being positioned semi-Fowler's with the shoulder and lap belts secured.
Your breathing pattern remained normal through all reassessments while en route.
We initially transferred you in a sitting position via a stairchair.
You were then placed semi-Fowler's on the stretcher for transport to the ambulance.
No changes in your mental status or coordination were detected at any time during the process.
We assisted you from the couch to the stretcher without incident.
Have you noted any swelling in your legs or feet beyond your usual condition?
No unusual swelling. I have my condition, but that was it.
All transport maneuvers were completed without issue or complaint from you.
Hospital was informed of your condition, and we're ready with the full report.
You were transported in a semi-Fowler's position from the house to the ambulance as discussed.
The destination facility now has our real-time updates and pre-arrival summary.
Your shortness of breath was resolved quickly, and no accessory muscles were used.
You have been transferred to ED room 21 for care.
Your mental status was alert and oriented throughout the entire emergency.
We found no signs of JVD, and no use of accessory muscles during chest pain episode.
No loss of consciousness or changes in responsiveness occurred during the encounter.
